claims.evidence_text: Adjusted genotype means for the VPD breakpoints ranged from 2.75 kPa to 4.1 kPa. A lower breakpoint might suggest a more conservative use of water.
claims.claim_summary: In 79 winter wheat lines, the VPD breakpoint above which transpiration is restricted ranged from 2.75 to 4.1 kPa.

claims.evidence_text: For bread wheat ( Triticum aestivum L.), differences in the VPD breakpoint between various wheat lines were detected in a range of 2.4–3.9 kPa
claims.claim_summary: Reported bread wheat VPD breakpoints for transpiration restriction range 2.4-3.9 kPa; durum wheat much lower (~1 kPa).

claims.evidence_text: analysis of variance (ANOVA) revealed highly significant genotype effects on cumulative transpiration ( p = 0.002) and TE ( p = < 0.001), ranging between 62.11 and 89.86 kg and 2.12–3.00 g kg − 1 , respectively
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat transpiration efficiency (biomass/grain per water transpired) ranged from 2.12 to 3.00 g/kg across genotypes.

claims.evidence_text: 48 plants per container are equivalent to 300 plants m − 2
claims.claim_summary: Field-like winter wheat plant density of 300 plants/m2 was used in the study.

claims.evidence_text: the limitation of transpiration rate in response to increasing VPD ... To prevent excessive water loss ... the plant is able to close its stomata, which in turn causes a decrease in photosynthetic rate.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat restricts transpiration by closing stomata at high VPD (above the breakpoint), conserving soil water but reducing photosynthetic rate; a water-conservation strategy beneficial under drought.
